title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
This project focuses on predicting customer churn using data analytics and machine learning techniques.


Customer churn is a critical problem for subscription-based businesses. In this project, we build predictive models to identify customers who are likely to leave and explore key drivers behind churn behavior.




Predict whether a customer will churn
Identify key drivers of churn
Provide actionable insights to reduce churn
Evaluate business impact of different strategies





Python
pandas
Machine Learning models
Data Visualization





Customers with low engagement are more likely to churn
Usage patterns strongly predict churn behavior
Machine learning models improve prediction accuracy




👉 View Full Project on GitHub

title: My Experience Using Obsidian for Note-Taking
text: Recently, I started using Obsidian as a note-taking tool to organize my learning. Compared to traditional tools like Word or Google Docs, Obsidian offers a more flexible and structured way to manage information.

title: My Experience Using Obsidian for Note-Taking
text: Recently, I started using Obsidian as a note-taking tool to organize my learning. Compared to traditional tools like Word or Google Docs, Obsidian offers a more flexible and structured way to manage information.

title: My Experience Using Obsidian for Note-Taking
text:
Key Features I Like
One of the most useful features in Obsidian is the ability to create links between notes. This allows me to connect ideas across different topics, which makes my notes feel more like a network rather than isolated pages.
I also find the Markdown-based system very convenient. It keeps everything simple and focused on content rather than formatting.

title: My Experience Using Obsidian for Note-Taking
text:
How I Use It
I mainly use Obsidian for:

Organizing class notes
Recording ideas from readings or videos
Structuring long-term learning topics

For example, I can create separate notes for finance, analytics, and personal development, and then link related concepts together.

title: My Experience Using Obsidian for Note-Taking
text:
Limitations
Although Obsidian is powerful, it does have a learning curve. It takes some time to understand how to organize notes effectively, especially when using features like backlinks and graph view.

title: My Experience Using Obsidian for Note-Taking
text:
Reflection
Overall, Obsidian has improved how I manage my learning process. It encourages me to think more actively about connections between ideas, rather than just storing information.

title: Building My First Quarto Website
text: In this project, I built my first multi-page website using Quarto. Before this, I had very little experience with web development, so the process was both challenging and rewarding.

title: Building My First Quarto Website
text: In this project, I built my first multi-page website using Quarto. Before this, I had very little experience with web development, so the process was both challenging and rewarding.

title: Building My First Quarto Website
text:
What I built
The website includes several sections such as an About page, a Resume page, a Projects page, and a Blog page. One of the most interesting parts was learning how these pages are connected through the _quarto.yml file.
I also added a blog section to make the website feel more dynamic and less static.

title: Building My First Quarto Website
text:
What I learned
Through this process, I learned:

How Quarto organizes content using .qmd files
How to create a navigation bar across multiple pages
How to customize the appearance of a website using CSS
How to structure content so that it is easy to read and navigate

title: Building My First Quarto Website
text:
Challenges
One of the main challenges I faced was adjusting the layout and styles. Small changes in CSS sometimes had unexpected effects, and I had to experiment a lot to understand how different elements interact.

title: Building My First Quarto Website
text:
Reflection
Overall, this project helped me understand how a simple text-based system can be used to build a complete website. It also gave me more confidence in learning new tools independently.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
My website includes a landing page where visitors can sign up to receive a newsletter. Since the landing page is often the first interaction a visitor has with the site, even small design or wording choices may affect whether that visitor decides to subscribe.
In this post, I study whether the wording of a call to action can change sign-up behavior. I compare two versions of the message shown on the page. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” while CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” Both messages are trying to encourage the same action, but they may not be equally effective.
To evaluate the two messages, I use an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to one of two versions of a webpage element. One group sees CTA A and the other sees CTA B. Because the assignment is random, differences in sign-up rates between the two groups can be attributed to the CTA wording rather than to systematic differences between visitors. The goal is to determine which CTA produces the higher newsletter sign-up rate.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
My website includes a landing page where visitors can sign up to receive a newsletter. Since the landing page is often the first interaction a visitor has with the site, even small design or wording choices may affect whether that visitor decides to subscribe.
In this post, I study whether the wording of a call to action can change sign-up behavior. I compare two versions of the message shown on the page. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” while CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” Both messages are trying to encourage the same action, but they may not be equally effective.
To evaluate the two messages, I use an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to one of two versions of a webpage element. One group sees CTA A and the other sees CTA B. Because the assignment is random, differences in sign-up rates between the two groups can be attributed to the CTA wording rather than to systematic differences between visitors. The goal is to determine which CTA produces the higher newsletter sign-up rate.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
We can express this A/B test as a simple statistical problem. For each visitor, the outcome is binary: either the visitor signs up for the newsletter or does not. Because the outcome only takes the values 1 or 0, each observation can be modeled as a Bernoulli random variable with parameter (), where () represents the probability of signing up.
The CTA shown to a visitor may affect that probability, so I allow the sign-up rate to differ across the two groups. Let (_A) be the probability of signing up for visitors who see CTA A, and let (_B) be the corresponding probability for visitors who see CTA B.
The quantity of interest is the difference in sign-up rates: [ = _A - _B ] If (> 0), CTA A performs better on average. If (< 0), CTA B performs better.
Because the true probabilities (_A) and (_B) are unknown, I estimate them using the observed sample means in each group. Since the data are binary, each sample mean is just the sample proportion of visitors who signed up. This gives the estimator [ = {X}_A - {X}_B ] where ({X}_A) is the sample sign-up rate for CTA A and ({X}_B) is the sample sign-up rate for CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real A/B test, the true sign-up probabilities would be unknown. We would only observe sample outcomes and then try to infer the underlying difference between the two CTAs. For this exercise, however, simulation is helpful because it lets us work in a setting where the truth is known in advance. That makes it easier to evaluate how well our estimator performs.
I assume that visitors who see CTA A sign up with probability (_A = 0.22), while visitors who see CTA B sign up with probability (_B = 0.18). This means the true treatment effect is [ = _A - _B = 0.04. ]
Using these assumptions, I simulate 1,000 Bernoulli observations for each group. Each observation equals 1 if the visitor signs up and 0 otherwise. These simulated samples will be used throughout the rest of the post.

pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
n_A = 1000
n_B = 1000

A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n_A)
B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n_B)

theta_true = pi_A - pi_B
theta_hat = A.mean() - B.mean()

print(f"Sample sign-up rate for CTA A: {A.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Sample sign-up rate for CTA B: {B.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Estimated difference (theta_hat): {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"True difference (theta): {theta_true:.4f}")

Sample sign-up rate for CTA A: 0.2160
Sample sign-up rate for CTA B: 0.1780
Estimated difference (theta_hat): 0.0380
True difference (theta): 0.0400

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers states that as the sample size increases, the sample average converges to the true population mean. In other words, while individual observations are noisy, averaging over many observations produces a more stable and reliable estimate.
In the context of this A/B test, this idea is important because our estimator of interest, ( = {X}_A - {X}_B), is based on sample averages. As we collect more data, we expect this estimate to get closer to the true treatment effect.
To illustrate this, I compute the difference between the outcomes in the two groups for each observation and then track how the cumulative average of these differences evolves as the sample size grows.

diffs = A - B
running_mean = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, len(diffs) + 1)

plt.figure()
plt.plot(np.arange(1, len(diffs) + 1), running_mean)
plt.axhline(theta_true, linestyle="--")
plt.xlabel("Number of paired observations")
plt.ylabel("Cumulative average of A - B")
plt.title("The Law of Large Numbers for the Estimated Treatment Effect")
plt.show()








At the beginning, the running average fluctuates substantially, even taking on extreme values, because each new observation has a large impact when the sample size is very small. As more observations are added, these fluctuations become less pronounced and the estimate begins to stabilize.
Over time, the cumulative average settles near the true treatment effect of 0.04. This illustrates the Law of Large Numbers: although individual observations are highly variable, averaging over a large number of observations produces a stable and reliable estimate of the underlying population parameter.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
So far, I have computed a single estimate of the treatment effect. However, this estimate is based on a finite sample and is therefore subject to randomness. If I were to repeat the experiment with a different sample, I would likely obtain a slightly different estimate.
To quantify this uncertainty, I use the concept of a standard error. The standard error measures how much an estimator is expected to vary across repeated samples. In this section, I estimate the standard error of () using a bootstrap approach and compare it to the analytical formula.
The bootstrap method works by repeatedly resampling from the observed data with replacement and recomputing the estimator each time. This produces an empirical distribution of the estimator, which can be used to approximate its variability.

n_boot = 1000
boot_thetas = []

for _ in range(n_boot):
    A_boot = np.random.choice(A, size=len(A), replace=True)
    B_boot = np.random.choice(B, size=len(B), replace=True)
    boot_theta = A_boot.mean() - B_boot.mean()
    boot_thetas.append(boot_theta)

boot_thetas = np.array(boot_thetas)


plt.hist(boot_thetas, bins=30)
plt.axvline(theta_hat, linestyle="--")
plt.xlabel("Bootstrap estimates of theta")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.title("Bootstrap Distribution of the Treatment Effect")
plt.show()








The histogram shows the distribution of the estimated treatment effect across many bootstrap samples. While each individual estimate differs slightly due to random sampling, the values are concentrated around the original estimate. This distribution reflects the uncertainty associated with ().

se_boot = boot_thetas.std()
print(f"Bootstrap standard error: {se_boot:.4f}")

Bootstrap standard error: 0.0177



se_analytical = np.sqrt(
    A.mean() * (1 - A.mean()) / len(A) +
    B.mean() * (1 - B.mean()) / len(B)
)

print(f"Analytical standard error: {se_analytical:.4f}")

Analytical standard error: 0.0178


The bootstrap standard error is very close to the analytical standard error. This is reassuring, as it suggests that the bootstrap procedure is capturing the variability of the estimator correctly. The analytical formula is derived under standard assumptions, while the bootstrap provides a more general, data-driven way to estimate uncertainty.

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot

print(f"95% CI: [{ci_lower:.4f}, {ci_upper:.4f}]")

95% CI: [0.0034, 0.0726]


Using the bootstrap standard error, I construct a 95% confidence interval for the treatment effect. This interval provides a range of plausible values for the true difference in sign-up rates. If the experiment were repeated many times, about 95% of such intervals would contain the true treatment effect. Importantly, the interval does not include zero, which suggests that there is a statistically significant difference between the two CTAs.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) provides a theoretical foundation for statistical inference. It states that, under fairly general conditions, the sampling distribution of an estimator becomes approximately normal as the sample size grows.
In this context, the estimator of interest is ( = {X}_A - {X}_B). Even though the underlying data are binary, the CLT tells us that the distribution of () across repeated samples should be approximately normal when the sample size is large.
To illustrate this, I repeatedly simulate new datasets and compute () each time. This allows me to approximate the sampling distribution of the estimator and compare it to a normal distribution.

n_sim = 1000
theta_hats = []

for _ in range(n_sim):
    A_sim = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n_A)
    B_sim = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n_B)
    theta_sim = A_sim.mean() - B_sim.mean()
    theta_hats.append(theta_sim)

theta_hats = np.array(theta_hats)


mean_theta = theta_hats.mean()
std_theta = theta_hats.std()

x = np.linspace(mean_theta - 4*std_theta, mean_theta + 4*std_theta, 100)

plt.hist(theta_hats, bins=30, density=True)
plt.plot(x, norm.pdf(x, mean_theta, std_theta))
plt.axvline(theta_true, linestyle="--")

plt.xlabel("Estimated treatment effect")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.title("Sampling Distribution of the Estimator and Normal Approximation")
plt.show()








The histogram shows the distribution of the estimated treatment effect across many repeated samples. The distribution is approximately symmetric and bell-shaped, even though the underlying data are binary.
The overlaid normal curve closely matches the simulated distribution, indicating that the normal approximation works well in this setting. This is consistent with the Central Limit Theorem, which states that the sampling distribution of an estimator becomes approximately normal as the sample size increases.
This result is important because it allows us to use normal-based methods, such as confidence intervals and hypothesis tests, even when the original data are not normally distributed.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
To formally evaluate whether the two CTAs perform differently, I use a hypothesis test. The goal is to assess whether the observed difference in sign-up rates could plausibly be explained by random variation alone.
The null hypothesis assumes that there is no difference between the two CTAs: [ H_0: = 0 ] while the alternative hypothesis is that there is a nonzero difference: [ H_1: . ]
Under the null hypothesis, the estimated treatment effect should be close to zero, up to sampling variability. I use the standard error of the estimator to compute a test statistic, which measures how far the observed estimate is from zero in standardized units.

z_stat = theta_hat / se_boot
print(f"Test statistic (z): {z_stat:.4f}")

Test statistic (z): 2.1529



p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))
print(f"P-value: {p_value:.4f}")

P-value: 0.0313


The p-value measures how likely it would be to observe a difference at least as extreme as the one in the data if the null hypothesis were true. In this case, the p-value is approximately 0.031, which is below the 5% significance level.
Therefore, I reject the null hypothesis of no difference between the two CTAs. This suggests that the observed difference in sign-up rates is unlikely to be due to random chance alone.
Since the estimated treatment effect is positive, the results indicate that CTA A leads to a higher sign-up rate than CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
Another way to analyze the A/B test is to frame it as a regression problem. Instead of comparing group means directly, I can estimate the treatment effect using a simple linear regression.
To do this, I combine the data from both groups into a single dataset and create an indicator variable that equals 1 if the observation comes from CTA A and 0 if it comes from CTA B. I then regress the binary outcome (sign-up or not) on this indicator.
In this setup, the coefficient on the indicator variable represents the difference in average outcomes between the two groups. This means that the regression coefficient should be equal to the estimator ( = {X}_A - {X}_B).
This shows that a two-sample t-test is equivalent to a regression with a binary treatment variable.

# Combine data
Y = np.concatenate([A, B])
T = np.concatenate([np.ones(len(A)), np.zeros(len(B))])


X = sm.add_constant(T)
model = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit()

coef = model.params[1]
se_reg = model.bse[1]
p_reg = model.pvalues[1]
intercept = model.params[0]

print(f"Intercept (mean outcome for CTA B): {intercept:.4f}")
print(f"Regression estimate of treatment effect: {coef:.4f}")
print(f"Standard error: {se_reg:.4f}")
print(f"P-value: {p_reg:.4f}")
print(f"Difference in means (theta_hat): {theta_hat:.4f}")

Intercept (mean outcome for CTA B): 0.1780
Regression estimate of treatment effect: 0.0380
Standard error: 0.0178
P-value: 0.0327
Difference in means (theta_hat): 0.0380



beta = model.params[1]
print(f"Regression estimate of treatment effect: {beta:.4f}")
print(f"Difference in means (theta_hat): {theta_hat:.4f}")

Regression estimate of treatment effect: 0.0380
Difference in means (theta_hat): 0.0380


The regression results match the earlier difference-in-means calculation exactly: the estimated coefficient on the treatment indicator is 0.038, which is the same as (). The intercept is 0.178, which corresponds to the average sign-up rate for CTA B.
This confirms that a simple regression with a binary treatment indicator recovers the same treatment effect as the two-sample comparison of means. In this sense, the t-test and the regression approach are just two different ways of expressing the same underlying statistical idea.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
A key assumption behind the hypothesis testing framework used above is that the data are analyzed only once, after the full sample has been collected. In practice, however, it is tempting to monitor the results of an experiment as data come in and stop early if the results look promising. This behavior is often referred to as “peeking.”
Peeking creates a problem because it effectively increases the number of opportunities to reject the null hypothesis. Each time we check the data, there is a chance of observing a statistically significant result purely by random variation. As a result, repeatedly checking the data inflates the probability of a false positive.
In other words, even if there is no true effect, peeking can make it appear as though there is one. This undermines the validity of p-values and confidence intervals, which rely on a fixed sampling plan.
To maintain valid inference, researchers must either commit to a pre-specified sample size or use methods that explicitly account for sequential testing. This highlights an important limitation of standard A/B testing procedures and underscores the need for careful experimental design.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Does raising the minimum wage cause unemployment? Standard economics textbooks say yes: if you force employers to pay workers more, they will simply hire fewer of them. Card and Krueger (1994) challenged this prediction head-on using a remarkably clean natural experiment.
On April 1, 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05 per hour — the highest state minimum wage in the country at the time. Pennsylvania, right next door, kept its minimum wage unchanged at $4.25. Card and Krueger surveyed 410 fast-food restaurants (Burger King, KFC, Roy Rogers, and Wendy’s) in both states before the increase (February–March 1992) and after (November–December 1992).
This setup is the classic Difference-in-Differences (DiD) design:

Treatment group: New Jersey restaurants (subject to the wage increase)
Control group: Pennsylvania restaurants (no change)
Key assumption: Without the minimum wage increase, employment trends in both states would have moved in parallel

The DiD estimator subtracts two differences:
\[
\hat{\tau}_{DiD}
=
(\bar{Y}_{NJ,after} - \bar{Y}_{NJ,before})
-
(\bar{Y}_{PA,after} - \bar{Y}_{PA,before})
\]
By comparing the change in NJ to the change in PA, we difference out any seasonal trends or macroeconomic shocks that affected both states equally — leaving only the effect of the minimum wage.
Their headline finding: employment in New Jersey did not fall after the minimum wage increase. In fact, it rose slightly relative to Pennsylvania, directly contradicting the standard competitive model.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Does raising the minimum wage cause unemployment? Standard economics textbooks say yes: if you force employers to pay workers more, they will simply hire fewer of them. Card and Krueger (1994) challenged this prediction head-on using a remarkably clean natural experiment.
On April 1, 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05 per hour — the highest state minimum wage in the country at the time. Pennsylvania, right next door, kept its minimum wage unchanged at $4.25. Card and Krueger surveyed 410 fast-food restaurants (Burger King, KFC, Roy Rogers, and Wendy’s) in both states before the increase (February–March 1992) and after (November–December 1992).
This setup is the classic Difference-in-Differences (DiD) design:

Treatment group: New Jersey restaurants (subject to the wage increase)
Control group: Pennsylvania restaurants (no change)
Key assumption: Without the minimum wage increase, employment trends in both states would have moved in parallel

The DiD estimator subtracts two differences:
\[
\hat{\tau}_{DiD}
=
(\bar{Y}_{NJ,after} - \bar{Y}_{NJ,before})
-
(\bar{Y}_{PA,after} - \bar{Y}_{PA,before})
\]
By comparing the change in NJ to the change in PA, we difference out any seasonal trends or macroeconomic shocks that affected both states equally — leaving only the effect of the minimum wage.
Their headline finding: employment in New Jersey did not fall after the minimum wage increase. In fact, it rose slightly relative to Pennsylvania, directly contradicting the standard competitive model.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Main Analysis

Table 2 — Key Variable Means (Partial Replication)
Table 2 in the paper reports descriptive statistics for both states before and after the minimum wage increase. We replicate the employment and wage rows below. The point of this table is to show that NJ and PA stores were similar before the policy — especially that their starting wages were nearly identical (NJ: $4.61, PA: $4.63), which validates the parallel trends assumption.


Wave 1 Variables

Our replicated means match the paper exactly. Notice that by Wave 2, the average starting wage in NJ jumped to $5.08 (just above the new $5.05 minimum), while PA wages barely moved. This confirms the policy was binding in NJ.


Table 3 — Difference-in-Differences (Rows 1–3, Columns i–iii)
This is the heart of the paper. We compare average FTE employment before and after the minimum wage increase in NJ vs. PA.

How to read this table:

Before the wage increase, NJ stores employed slightly fewer workers than PA stores (20.44 vs. 23.33).
After the increase, NJ employment rose while PA employment fell.
The DiD estimate of +2.76 FTE employees (our replication: +2.75) is positive and statistically significant (t ≈ 2.03), directly contradicting the prediction that raising the minimum wage reduces employment.



Table 4, Column (i) — Regression-Adjusted DiD
A simple OLS regression of the change in FTE employment on a New Jersey dummy confirms the DiD result while being more precise about the standard error. The sample here is restricted to stores with valid employment and wage data in both waves.


Sample size:        351  (paper: 357)
NJ dummy (β):       2.28  (paper: 2.33)
Standard error:     1.19  (paper: 1.19)
t-statistic:        1.91
Mean ΔemploymentPA: -1.88


The coefficient on the NJ dummy (2.28, paper: 2.33) tells us that, controlling for nothing else, NJ stores gained about 2.3 more FTE employees than PA stores between Wave 1 and Wave 2. The standard error of 1.19 matches the paper exactly. The small discrepancy in the point estimate likely reflects minor differences in handling temporarily closed stores.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
section: Something Additional: Visualizing the Wage Distribution Shift
text:
Something Additional: Visualizing the Wage Distribution Shift
One of the most compelling pieces of evidence in the paper is Figure 1: the distribution of starting wages in NJ and PA before and after the minimum wage increase. The paper only shows this as a histogram — here we recreate and extend it to make the Diff-in-Diff logic visually intuitive.
The key insight: if the minimum wage is truly binding (i.e., it forced real wage changes), we should see NJ’s wage distribution shift dramatically toward $5.05 after April 1992, while PA’s distribution stays flat. This is exactly what happened — and it’s the “first stage” that validates the whole study.





Starting wage distributions before and after New Jersey’s minimum wage increase. The policy dramatically compressed NJ wages upward to the new $5.05 floor, while Pennsylvania’s distribution barely changed.






Share of stores paying exactly $5.05 (new minimum):
  NJ Wave 2: 89.0%  (paper: 85.2%)
  PA Wave 2: 1.4%  (paper:  1.3%)

Share of stores still paying $4.25 (old minimum):
  NJ Wave 1: 32.2%  (paper: 30.5%)
  PA Wave 1: 34.2%  (paper: 32.9%)
  NJ Wave 2: 0.0%  (paper:  0.0%)
  PA Wave 2: 28.2%  (paper: 25.3%)


What this shows:

Before the increase (Wave 1), NJ and PA had nearly identical wage distributions — about 30% of stores in both states paid exactly the old minimum of $4.25/hr. This similarity supports the parallel-trends assumption underlying the DiD design.
After the increase (Wave 2), 85% of NJ stores piled up right at the new $5.05 floor, while Pennsylvania’s distribution barely moved. By Wave 2, zero NJ stores still paid $4.25.
This dramatic shift is the “first stage” of the causal chain: the law actually changed wages, so any subsequent employment effects can be attributed to the policy rather than coincidence.
Despite this large wage shock, NJ employment increased relative to PA — the central paradox that motivated the paper and challenged a generation of economists to rethink the competitive model of the labor market.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
References
Card, David, and Alan B. Krueger. 1994. “Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast-Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania.” American Economic Review 84 (4): 772–793.

title: A Simple Marketing Analysis: Why Branding Matters
text: In marketing, branding plays an important role in shaping how customers perceive a product. Even when two products have similar features, strong branding can significantly influence purchasing decisions.

title: A Simple Marketing Analysis: Why Branding Matters
text: In marketing, branding plays an important role in shaping how customers perceive a product. Even when two products have similar features, strong branding can significantly influence purchasing decisions.

title: A Simple Marketing Analysis: Why Branding Matters
text:
Example
For example, consider two coffee shops offering similar drinks at similar prices. One has a well-designed logo, consistent visual identity, and a clear brand story, while the other does not.
Most customers are more likely to choose the brand that appears more professional and trustworthy.

title: A Simple Marketing Analysis: Why Branding Matters
text:
Key Insight
Branding is not just about design. It includes:

Visual identity (logo, colors, layout)
Messaging and tone
Customer experience

All of these elements work together to create a consistent image in the customer’s mind.

title: A Simple Marketing Analysis: Why Branding Matters
text:
Why It Matters
Strong branding can:

Increase customer trust
Improve perceived value
Differentiate a product from competitors

This is especially important in competitive markets.

title: A Simple Marketing Analysis: Why Branding Matters
text:
Reflection
This simple example shows that marketing is not only about the product itself, but also about how the product is presented. Understanding this can help businesses make better strategic decisions.

title: Pentathlon: Next Product to Buy Modeling
text:
This project develops customer-level predictive models to optimize promotional messaging strategies using experimental e-mail marketing data.


The goal of this project is to move from a one-size-fits-all marketing approach to a personalized decision framework that maximizes long-term customer value.
A randomized controlled trial (RCT) tested different e-mail frequencies (1–4 per week). While higher frequency increased short-term revenue, it also led to higher unsubscribe rates, reducing long-term customer lifetime value (LTV).
This project builds on that experimental data to determine the most effective message (or no message) for each individual customer.




Feature engineering using Polars
Built multiple predictive models:

Logistic Regression
Random Forest
XGBoost
Neural Networks

Applied the Next Product to Buy (NPTB) framework
Estimated:

Purchase probability
Expected order value
Expected profit






Customer behavior varies significantly, making personalization critical
Higher email frequency increases short-term revenue but harms long-term engagement
Predictive modeling enables better targeting decisions than static strategies
Personalized messaging significantly improves expected profit per customer





Built and compared multiple predictive models
Engineered features and processed data
Evaluated model performance using multiple metrics
Interpreted results and translated them into business insights




👉 View Full Project on GitHub
